lessons.markdown:
## Asking "how are you?"

Welcome to **Coffee Break French**, the course aimed at independent learners of French which will introduce you to the French language in easy steps. These program notes will help you get more out of each episode by explaining how words are written and giving you more information about each phrase along with some bonus vocabulary where appropriate. 

---

## 🔤 Language notes

### oui
> *yes*
> The French word for 'yes' is pronounced like Scottish "wee".

### non
> *no*
> Remember to get the nasal sound here - if it helps, hold your nose!

### What's up?
> *how are you?*
> Just as in English, there are many ways of asking someone in French how they're feeling. This is the simplest way and it literally means "that's going?", as in "things are going well?"
> Note also that the word **ça** has a diacritic mark on the 'c'. It's like a very small comma or number 5 hanging down from the 'c'. It's called a **cedilla** and it makes the 'c' sound like an 's'. If there was no cedilla in the word *ça* it would sound like "ca-" in "car".

### ça va
> *"things are fine"*
> Note that you can answer the question *ça va?* by saying *ça va*. The difference is that with the question you raise your voice towards the end of the sentence, and with the statement there's no change in intonation.

###bien
> *well*
> Note the nasal sound in the recording.

### What's the matter?
> *things are going well*
> Note that *bien* means "well" as opposed to "good". If you learn the word as meaning "well" it should save you some confusion later!

### merci
> *thank you*
> Take care with the French 'r' sound. It's like a guttural, rolled 'r', quite difficult to produce for non-native speakers.

### ça va bien, merci
> *things are going well, thanks*; "I'm fine, thanks"

### et toi?
> *and you?; what about you?*
> *Toi* is the informal form of "you" in this phrase. There is a formal form which we'll cover in future episodes.

###très
> *very*
> Again, watch the French 'r' in this word.

### Find out more
> *things are going very well*

### ça va très bien, merci
> *things are going very well, thank you*

### excellent
> *excellent*
> Although the word looks identical to the English word, it's pronounced in French.

### What's new
> *things aren't going well*
> To make something negative in French you put *ne ... pas* round the verb. There will be further discussion of this in future episodes.

### je suis en forme
> *I'm feeling good; I'm on form*
> *Je suis* means "I am" and is a phrase we'll be coming back to many times. *En form* literally means "on form".

### je suis fatigué
> *I'm tired (masculine)*
> The word *fatigué* comes from the same root as the English word "fatigue", meaning "tiredness".

### je suis fatiguée
> *I'm tired (feminine)*
> There is a feminine form of *fatigué* which has an extra 'e' on the end. This makes no difference to the pronunciation, but it's vital to include the extra 'e' when you're writing in French.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: ç é è

We mentioned earlier that French uses a **cedilla** to make a 'c' sound like an 's', as in the word *ça*. French also uses other accents, or "diacritic marks". In this lesson we've come across:

| Accent | Name | Example | Pronunciation |
|--------|------|---------|--------------|
| **ç** | cedilla | *ca* | makes 'c' sound like 's' |
| **é** | acute accent | *fatigue* | sounds like "ay" in "hay" |
| **è** | grave accent | *très* | sounds like "e" in "egg" |

Both these accents affect how a word is pronounced.

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

Where appropriate we'll include some bonus vocabulary which will help to extend the range of language covered in each lesson.

### comment ça va?
> *how are things?*
> This is the 'full' version of the shortened phrase *ça va?*

### What's the rule?
> *how's it going?*
> A very colloquial way of asking "how are things?" This phrase should be kept for close friends only and tends to be used more among young people.

###genial
> *great*
> You're more likely to say this as a one-word answer, rather than say *ça va génial* which sounds a little strange.

### comme ci, comme ça
> *so so*
> In between *ça va bien* and *ça ne va pas*.

### je ne suis pas en forme
> *I'm not feeling good; I'm not on form*
> The negative version of *je suis en forme*: note the *ne ... pas* going round the verb (*suis*).

### bof
> *almost untranslatable!*
> This is a very French expression and virtually always is accompanied by a shrug of the shoulders and/or raised eyebrows. If you ask someone *ça va* and they reply *bof* then basically it means things aren't great but they've accepted that things are never that great anyway...

---

## Lesson 2: Introducing Yourself

Welcome back to **Coffee Break French**! In this lesson, you'll learn how to introduce yourself in French — sharing your name, where you're from, and how to politely respond when meeting someone new.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Salut
> *hi / bye (informal)*
> Use with friends or people your age. Works for both "hello" and "goodbye".

### Comment tu t'appelles ?
> *What's your name? (informal)*
> Literally: "How do you call yourself?". The verb *s'appeler* is reflexive.

### Je m'appelle...
> *My name is...*
> Example: *Je m'appelle Sophie*. The *m'* is a shortened form of *me* before a vowel.

### Enchanté / Enchantée
> *Nice to meet you*
> Add an extra 'e' if you are female (*enchantée*). Pronunciation is the same.

### Tu viens d'où ?
> *Where are you from? (informal)*
> *Venir* means "to come". *D'où* = "from where".

### Je viens de...
> *I am from...*
> Examples: *Je viens de Paris*, *Je viens d'Angleterre* (use *d'* before a vowel).

### au revoir
> *goodbye*
> A polite, universal way to say goodbye. Pronounced: "oh ruh-vwar".

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: French Gender

French words have **gender**: masculine or feminine. This affects spelling.

| Masculine | Feminine | Example |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| enchanté | enchantée | Nice to meet you |
| français | française | French (person) |
| fatigué | fatiguée | tired |

💡 **Tip**: Learn new nouns with their article: *le livre* (masc.), *la table* (fem.).

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **Et toi ? / Et vous ?** — *And you? (informal / formal)*
- **Je suis...** — *I am...* (e.g., *Je suis étudiant*)
- **un peu** — *a little* (e.g., *Je parle un peu français*)
- **s'il te plaît / s'il vous plaît** — *please*
- **Comment ça s'écrit ?** — *How do you spell that?*

## Lesson 3: Numbers 1-20 and Asking Age

Welcome to Lesson 3! Today you'll learn to count from 1 to 20 in French and how to ask and tell someone's age — essential skills for everyday conversations.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### un, deux, trois
> *one, two, three*
> Pronounced: uh, duh, twah. Note that the final consonants in *deux* and *trois* are silent.

### quatre, cinq, six
> *four, five, six*
> Pronounced: katr, sank, sees. *Cinq* has a nasal sound. *Six* is pronounced "sees" when alone.

### sept, huit, neuf, dix
> *seven, eight, nine, ten*
> Pronounced: set, weet, nuhf, dees. *Huit* sounds like "weet" with a soft 'h'.

### onze, douze, treize
> *eleven, twelve, thirteen*
> Pronounced: onz, dooz, trez. These follow a similar pattern to English.

### quatorze, quinze, seize
> *fourteen, fifteen, sixteen*
> Pronounced: kah-torz,kanz, sez. *Quinze* has a nasal sound.

### dix-sept, dix-huit, dix-neuf, vingt
> *seventeen, eighteen, nineteen, twenty*
> Pronounced: dees-set, dees-weet, dees-nuhf, van. *Vingt* has a nasal sound and the 't' is silent.

### Quel âge as-tu ?
> *How old are you? (informal)*
> Literally: "What age do you have?" In French, you "have" an age, you don't "be" an age.

### J'ai ... ans
> *I am ... years old*
> Literally: "I have ... years." Example: *J'ai vingt-cinq ans* (I'm 25 years old).

### ans
> *years*
> Always use *ans* after a number when talking about age. Pronounced: ahn (nasal sound).

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: French Numbers

French numbers from 1-16 are unique words. From 17-19, they combine *dix* (10) + the number:
- 17 = *dix-sept* (10-7)
- 18 = *dix-huit* (10-8)
- 19 = *dix-neuf* (10-9)

**20 = vingt** (van)

💡 **Pronunciation tip**: French numbers often have silent final consonants. Focus on the vowel sounds!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **et un / et une** — *twenty-one* (literally "twenty and one")
- **Quel âge avez-vous ?** — *How old are you? (formal)*
- **J'ai ...** — *I have...* (used for age: *J'ai trente ans* = I'm 30)
- **tu as ... ans** — *you are ... years old* (literally: "you have ... years")
- **il/elle a ... ans** — *he/she is ... years old*

## Lesson 4: Days of the Week and Making Plans

Welcome to Lesson 4! Today you'll learn the days of the week in French and how to make simple plans — perfect for scheduling meetups or talking about your week.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### lundi, mardi, mercredi
> *Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday*
> Pronounced: lun-dee, mar-dee, mer-cre-di. Note the soft 'r' in *mercredi*.

### jeudi, vendredi, samedi
> *Thursday, Friday, Saturday*
> Pronounced: zhuh-dee, von-dree-dee, sam-dee. *Vendredi* has a nasal 'en' sound.

### dimanche
> *Sunday*
> Pronounced: dee-mahnsh. The 'ch' sounds like "sh" in "ship".

### aujourd'hui
> *today*
> Pronounced: oh-zhoor-dwee. Literally: "at this day".

### demain
> *tomorrow*
> Pronounced: duh-mahn. Nasal 'ain' sound.

### On se voit... ?
> *Shall we meet...? / See you...?*
> Example: *On se voit lundi ?* = "Shall we meet on Monday?" Informal and very common.

### le + [day]
> *on [day]*
> In French, use *le* before a day to mean "on": *le lundi* = "on Monday".

### à + [time]
> *at [time]*
> Example: *à dix heures* = "at 10 o'clock". We'll cover telling time in a future lesson.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: French Days

💡 **Important**: Days of the week in French are **not capitalized** unless they start a sentence.

| French | English | Pronunciation |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| lundi | Monday | lun-dee |
| mardi | Tuesday | mar-dee |
| mercredi | Wednesday | mer-cre-di |
| jeudi | Thursday | zhuh-dee |
| vendredi | Friday | von-dree-dee |
| samedi | Saturday | sam-dee |
| dimanche | Sunday | dee-mahnsh |

**Grammar tip**: To say "on Monday", use *le lundi*. To say "every Monday", also use *le lundi* — context tells the difference!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **la semaine** — *the week* (e.g., *la semaine prochaine* = next week)
- **le week-end** — *the weekend* (pronounced: "lee week-end")
- **Quand ?** — *When?* (simple and useful!)
- **Ça te dit... ?** — *How about...? / Does that sound good to you?* (informal)
- **D'accord** — *Okay / Agreed* (pronounced: dah-kor)

## Lesson 5: Telling Time and Scheduling

Welcome to Lesson 5! Today you'll learn how to tell time in French and make simple appointments — essential for daily life and planning your day.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Quelle heure est-il ?
> *What time is it?*
> Pronounced: kel ur ay-teel. The formal way to ask for the time.

### Il est ... heure(s)
> *It is ... o'clock*
> Example: *Il est trois heures* = "It's 3 o'clock". Use *heure* (singular) for 1, *heures* (plural) for 2+.

### une heure, deux heures, trois heures
> *one o'clock, two o'clock, three o'clock*
> Pronounced: oon ur, duh zur, twah zur. Note the liaison in *deux heures* and *trois heures*.

### midi / minuit
> *noon / midnight*
> Pronounced: mee-dee / mee-nwee. These stand alone — no *heures* needed.

### et quart / et demie / moins le quart
> *quarter past / half past / quarter to*
> Examples: *deux heures et quart* (2:15), *deux heures et demie* (2:30), *trois heures moins le quart* (2:45).

### du matin / de l'après-midi / du soir
> *in the morning / in the afternoon / in the evening*
> Add these to clarify time of day: *dix heures du soir* = 10 PM.

### à quelle heure ?
> *at what time?*
> Example: *À quelle heure on se voit ?* = "What time shall we meet?"

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Telling Time

💡 **Key rules**:
- Always use *Il est...* to tell time (never "C'est")
- *Heure* is feminine: use *une heure*, *deux heures*, etc.
- For "at [time]", use *à*: *à huit heures* = at 8 o'clock

| French | Time | Pronunciation |
|--------|------|---------------|
| Il est une heure | 1:00 | eel ay oon ur |
| Il est midi | 12:00 PM | eel ay mee-dee |
| Il est deux heures et demie | 2:30 | eel ay duh zur ay duh-mee |
| Il est cinq heures moins le quart | 4:45 | eel ay sank ur mwan luh kar |

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **pile** — *exactly / on the dot* (e.g., *dix heures pile* = 10:00 sharp)
- **environ** — *about / around* (e.g., *environ huit heures* = around 8)
- **le rendez-vous** — *appointment / meeting* (pronounced: "luh ron-day-voo")
- **en retard / à l'heure** — *late / on time*
- **C'est noté !** — *Noted! / Got it!* (casual confirmation)

quizPairs.question: une heure, deux heures, trois heures
quizPairs.answer: one o'clock, two o'clock, three o'clock

quizPairs.question: et quart / et demie / moins le quart
quizPairs.answer: quarter past / half past / quarter to

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 6: Common Verbs & Daily Routines

Welcome to Lesson 6! Today you'll learn essential French verbs and how to talk about your daily routine — a key step toward building real sentences.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### je / tu / il / elle
> *I / you (informal) / he / she*
> These are subject pronouns. Use them before verbs: *je parle* (I speak), *tu manges* (you eat).

### parler
> *to speak / to talk*
> Pronounced: par-lay. Example: *Je parle français* = I speak French.

### manger
> *to eat*
> Pronounced: mahn-zhay. Example: *Je mange une pomme* = I eat an apple.

### travailler
> *to work*
> Pronounced: trah-vah-yay. Example: *Je travaille le lundi* = I work on Monday.

### habiter
> *to live (in a place)*
> Pronounced: ah-bee-tay. Example: *J'habite à Paris* = I live in Paris.

### Je... / Tu... / Il...
> *I... / You... / He...*
> Conjugation tip: For regular -er verbs, drop -er and add: -e (je), -es (tu), -e (il/elle).

### le matin / l'après-midi / le soir
> *in the morning / afternoon / evening*
> Use these to describe when you do things: *Je travaille le matin* = I work in the morning.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Regular -er Verbs

💡 Most French verbs end in **-er** and follow a simple pattern:

| Pronoun | Ending | Example: parler |
|---------|--------|-----------------|
| je | -e | je parle |
| tu | -es | tu parles |
| il/elle | -e | il parle |
| nous | -ons | nous parlons |
| vous | -ez | vous parlez |
| ils/elles | -ent | ils parlent |

🗣️ Pronunciation tip: The endings -e, -es, -ent are usually silent! Focus on the stem.

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **se lever** — *to get up* (reflexive verb, we'll cover these later)
- **prendre** — *to take / to have* (e.g., *prendre un café* = to have a coffee)
- **aller** — *to go* (irregular verb — very important!)
- **faire** — *to do / to make* (another key irregular verb)
- **et toi ?** — *and you?* (to return a question)

## Lesson 7: Food & Ordering at a Café

Welcome to Lesson 7! Today you'll learn essential food vocabulary and how to order drinks and snacks in French — perfect for traveling or practicing real-life conversations.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### un café / un thé / une eau
> *a coffee / a tea / a water*
> Pronounced: uh kah-fay / uh tay / oon oh. Note: *eau* (water) is feminine.

### je voudrais...
> *I would like...*
> Pronounced: zhuh voo-dreh. The polite way to order. Example: *Je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît.*

### s'il vous plaît / s'il te plaît
> *please (formal / informal)*
> Pronounced: seel voo pleh / seel tuh pleh. Always add this when ordering!

### combien ça coûte ?
> *how much does it cost?*
> Pronounced: kohm-byahn sah koot. Essential for shopping and cafés.

### l'addition, s'il vous plaît
> *the bill, please*
> Pronounced: lah-dee-syohn, seel voo pleh. Use this when you're ready to pay.

### un croissant / une baguette / un sandwich
> *a croissant / a baguette / a sandwich*
> Pronounced: uh krwah-sahn / oon bah-get / uh sand-weesh. Classic French café items.

### C'est tout.
> *That's all.*
> Pronounced: say too. Say this when you're done ordering.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Gender of Food Words

💡 In French, every noun has a gender. This affects the article you use:

| Masculine (un) | Feminine (une) |
|----------------|----------------|
| un café | une eau |
| un thé | une baguette |
| un sandwich | une salade |
| un croissant | une crêpe |

🗣️ Tip: When in doubt, listen for *un* or *une* when learning new words!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **avoir faim / soif** — *to be hungry / thirsty* (literally: "to have hunger/thirst")
- **Je prends...** — *I'll have...* (casual alternative to *je voudrais*)
- **délicieux / délicieuse** — *delicious* (masc./fem.)
- **l'addition est pour moi** — *the bill is on me*
- **Bon appétit !** — *Enjoy your meal!* (common phrase before eating)

quizPairs.question: un café / un thé / une eau
quizPairs.answer: a coffee / a tea / a water

quizPairs.question: s'il vous plaît / s'il te plaît

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 8: Family & Describing People

Welcome to Lesson 8! Today you'll learn vocabulary for family members and basic adjectives to describe people — great for personal conversations and building sentences.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### la famille
> *the family*
> Pronounced: lah fah-mee. A feminine noun.

### mon père / ma mère
> *my father / my mother*
> Pronounced: mohN pehr / mah mehr. Note: *mon/ma* change based on the noun's gender.

### mon frère / ma sœur
> *my brother / my sister*
> Pronounced: mohN frehr / mah sur. *Sœur* has a unique vowel sound — like "ur" in "fur".

### un fils / une fille
> *a son / a daughter*
> Pronounced: uh fees / oon fee. Note: *fille* can also mean "girl".

### il est / elle est
> *he is / she is*
> Pronounced: eel ay / el ay. Use with adjectives: *Il est grand* = He is tall.

### grand / petite
> *tall / small (feminine)*
> Pronounced: grahn / puh-teet. Adjectives agree with the noun's gender.

### sympa
> *nice / friendly*
> Pronounced: sim-pah. Short for *sympathique*. Works for both genders!

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Possessive Adjectives

💡 In French, *my* changes based on the noun — not the owner:

| Noun Gender | My | Example |
|-------------|----|---------|
| Masculine | mon | mon père (my father) |
| Feminine | ma | ma mère (my mother) |
| Before vowel | mon | mon amie (my friend — f.) |

🗣️ Tip: Even if you're female, say *mon amie* (not *ma amie*) because *amie* starts with a vowel!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **les parents** — *parents* (pronounced: lay pah-rahn)
- **un enfant** — *a child* (masc., but works for any gender)
- **jeune / âgé(e)** — *young / old (person)*
- **il/elle a les yeux...** — *he/she has ... eyes* (e.g., *les yeux bleus* = blue eyes)
- **C'est qui ?** — *Who is it?* (casual way to ask about a person)

## Lesson 9: Shopping & Prices (Numbers 20-100)

Welcome to Lesson 9! Today you'll extend your number skills to 100 and learn essential phrases for shopping in French — practical for travel and everyday life.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### vingt, trente, quarante, cinquante
> *20, 30, 40, 50*
> Pronounced: van, trahnt, kah-raht, sank-ahnt. Note the nasal sounds in *trente* and *cinquante*.

### soixante, soixante-dix, quatre-vingts, cent
> *60, 70, 80, 100*
> Pronounced: swah-sahnt / swah-sahnt-dees / katr-vehn / sahn.
> 💡 French uses a 20-based system for 70-99: 70 = 60+10, 80 = 4x20, 90 = 4x20+10.

### Je cherche...
> *I'm looking for...*
> Pronounced: zhuh shersh. Example: *Je cherche un cadeau* = I'm looking for a gift.

### Où est... ?
> *Where is...?*
> Pronounced: oo ay. Example: *Où est la caisse ?* = Where is the checkout?

### Je le/la prends
> *I'll take it (masc./fem.)*
> Pronounced: zhuh luh prahn / zhuh lah prahn. Use *le* for masculine items, *la* for feminine.

### C'est combien ?
> *How much is it?*
> Pronounced: say kohm-byahn. Casual alternative to *combien ça coûte*.

### la caisse / le magasin
> *checkout / store*
> Pronounced: lah kess / luh mah-gah-zeh. Essential shopping locations.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Numbers 70-99

💡 French has a unique system for 70-99:

| Number | French | Literal Meaning |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| 70 | soixante-dix | 60 + 10 |
| 71 | soixante et onze | 60 + 11 |
| 80 | quatre-vingts | 4 × 20 |
| 81 | quatre-vingt-un | 4 × 20 + 1 |
| 90 | quatre-vingt-dix | 4 × 20 + 10 |
| 99 | quatre-vingt-dix-neuf | 4 × 20 + 19 |

🗣️ Tip: In Belgium/Switzerland, they use *septante* (70), *huitante* (80), *nonante* (90) — simpler!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **une boutique** — *a small shop / boutique*
- **cher / chère** — *expensive* (masc./fem.)
- **bon marché** — *inexpensive / good value*
- **Je peux essayer ?** — *Can I try it on?* (for clothes)
- **C'est parfait !** — *That's perfect!* (great for confirming a purchase)

## Lesson 10: Review & Simple Conversations

Welcome to Lesson 10 — your first milestone! Today we'll review key phrases from Lessons 1-9 and practice putting them together in simple, real-life conversations.

---

## 🔤 Review: Core Phrases

### Greetings & Basics
- Salut / Bonjour — hi / hello
- Comment ça va ? — how are you?
- Ça va bien, merci — I'm fine, thanks
- Au revoir — goodbye

### Introducing Yourself
- Je m'appelle... — my name is...
- Enchanté(e) — nice to meet you
- Je viens de... — I am from...

### Time & Plans
- Quel jour ? — what day?
- À quelle heure ? — at what time?
- On se voit... ? — shall we meet...?

### Shopping & Needs
- Je voudrais... — I would like...
- C'est combien ? — how much is it?
- Je cherche... — I'm looking for...

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Building Simple Sentences

💡 You already know enough to create basic sentences! Just combine:

**Subject + Verb + Object/Detail**

Examples:
- Je + m'appelle + Sophie → Je m'appelle Sophie.
- Tu + viens + de Paris → Tu viens de Paris.
- Je + voudrais + un café → Je voudrais un café.

🗣️ Tip: Don't worry about perfect grammar yet — focus on being understood!

---

## 🎁 Bonus: Conversation Starters

- **Et toi ? / Et vous ?** — *And you?* (to keep the conversation going)
- **Vraiment ?** — *Really?* (show interest)
- **Ah bon ?** — *Oh, really?* (casual surprise)
- **D'accord !** — *Okay!* (confirm understanding)
- **À bientôt !** — *See you soon!* (friendly goodbye)

## Lesson 11: Places in the City & Directions

Welcome to Lesson 11! Today you'll learn vocabulary for places in a city and how to ask for and give directions — essential for navigating French-speaking cities.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### la ville / le centre-ville
> *the city / the city center*
> Pronounced: lah veel / luh sahn-truh veel. *Centre-ville* is where you'll find most shops and cafés.

### Où est... ? / Où sont... ?
> *Where is...? (singular) / Where are...? (plural)*
> Pronounced: oo ay / oo sohn. Use *Où est* for one thing, *Où sont* for multiple things.

### à gauche / à droite / tout droit
> *to the left / to the right / straight ahead*
> Pronounced: ah gohsh / ah drwat / too drwah. Essential direction words!

### la rue / la place / le parc
> *the street / the square / the park*
> Pronounced: lah roo / lah plahs / luh park. Common places you'll encounter.

### près de / loin de
> *near / far from*
> Pronounced: preh duh / lwehn duh. Example: *près de la gare* = near the station.

### la gare / l'aéroport
> *the train station / the airport*
> Pronounced: lah gar / lah-ay-roh-por. Key transportation hubs.

### Excusez-moi...
> *Excuse me...*
> Pronounced: ex-kew-zay mwah. Polite way to start asking for directions.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Asking for Directions

💡 To ask where something is, use:

**Où est + [place] ?**

Examples:
- Où est la gare ? = Where is the train station?
- Où est le parc ? = Where is the park?
- Où est la poste ? = Where is the post office?

🗣️ To answer, use:
- **C'est par là** = It's that way
- **C'est près d'ici** = It's near here
- **C'est loin** = It's far

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **la poste** — *the post office*
- **la banque** — *the bank*
- **le musée** — *the museum*
- **l'église** — *the church* (feminine!)
- **Je suis perdu(e)** — *I'm lost* (masc./fem.)

## Lesson 12: Weather & Seasons

Welcome to Lesson 12! Today you'll learn how to talk about the weather and seasons in French — perfect for small talk and planning outdoor activities.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Quel temps fait-il ?
> *What's the weather like?*
> Pronounced: kel tahn feh-teel. The standard way to ask about weather.

### Il fait beau / chaud / froid
> *It's nice / hot / cold (weather)*
> Pronounced: eel feh boh / sho / frwah. Use *Il fait* for weather conditions.

### Il pleut / Il neige
> *It's raining / It's snowing*
> Pronounced: eel pluh / eel nezh. Two essential weather verbs.

### le soleil / la pluie / la neige
> *the sun / the rain / the snow*
> Pronounced: luh so-lay / lah plwee / lah nezh. Weather nouns.

### le printemps / l'été / l'automne / l'hiver
> *spring / summer / autumn / winter*
> Pronounced: luh pran-tahn / lay-teh / loh-tonn / lee-ver. The four seasons.

### aujourd'hui / demain
> *today / tomorrow*
> Pronounced: oh-zhoor-dwee / duh-mahn. Use with weather: *Aujourd'hui il fait froid*.

### en + [season]
> *in [season]*
> Example: *en été* = in summer, *en hiver* = in winter. Note: *au printemps* (not "en").

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Talking About Weather

💡 French uses **Il fait** + adjective for most weather:

| French | English |
|--------|---------|
| Il fait beau | It's nice/sunny |
| Il fait chaud | It's hot |
| Il fait froid | It's cold |
| Il fait doux | It's mild |

And **Il + verb** for precipitation:
- Il pleut = It's raining
- Il neige = It's snowing

🗣️ Note: *Il fait* literally means "It makes" but is used for weather!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **nuageux / nuageuse** — *cloudy* (masc./fem.)
- **il y a du soleil** — *there is sun / it's sunny*
- **quel temps de chien !** — *what awful weather!* (informal expression)
- **Il fait + number + degrés** — *It's + number + degrees* (e.g., *Il fait vingt degrés*)
- **J'aime / Je n'aime pas...** — *I like / I don't like...* (for seasons)

## Lesson 13: Hobbies & Free Time

Welcome to Lesson 13! Today you'll learn vocabulary for hobbies and leisure activities, plus how to talk about what you like to do in your free time.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### J'aime... / Je n'aime pas...
> *I like... / I don't like...*
> Pronounced: zhem / zhuh nem pah. The foundation for expressing preferences.

### le sport / la musique / la lecture
> *sports / music / reading*
> Pronounced: luh spor / lah mew-zeek / lah lek-tewr. Popular hobbies.

### jouer à... / jouer de...
> *to play (sport/game) / to play (instrument)*
> Pronounced: zhoo-eh ah / zhoo-eh duh. Use *à* for sports, *de* for instruments.

### regarder / écouter / lire
> *to watch / to listen / to read*
> Pronounced: ruh-gar-day / ay-koo-tay / leer. Common leisure verbs.

### le cinéma / le restaurant / le café
> *the cinema / the restaurant / the café*
> Pronounced: luh see-nay-mah / luh res-toh-rahn / luh kah-fay. Places for free time.

### avec mes amis
> *with my friends*
> Pronounced: ah-vek may zah-mee. Great for social activities.

### le week-end / le soir
> *on the weekend / in the evening*
> Pronounced: luh week-end / luh swar. When you do activities.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Expressing Likes

💡 To talk about what you enjoy:

**J'aime + [activity/noun]**

Examples:
- J'aime le sport. = I like sports.
- J'aime regarder des films. = I like watching movies.
- J'aime la musique. = I like music.

To say you don't like something:
**Je n'aime pas...**
- Je n'aime pas le football. = I don't like soccer.

🗣️ Tip: After *aimer*, use the definite article (*le, la, les*), not *un/une*!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **voyager** — *to travel*
- **cuisiner** — *to cook*
- **danser** — *to dance*
- **photographier** — *to take photos*
- **Qu'est-ce que tu aimes faire ?** — *What do you like to do?*

quizPairs.question: regarder / écouter / lire
quizPairs.answer: to watch / to listen / to read

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 14: At the Restaurant

Welcome to Lesson 14! Today you'll learn essential phrases for dining at a French restaurant — from making a reservation to ordering your meal and paying the bill.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Une table pour... personnes, s'il vous plaît
> *A table for... people, please*
> Pronounced: oon tahbl poor... pehr-sonn, seel voo pleh. How to reserve or request a table.

### la carte / le menu
> *the menu (à la carte) / the set menu*
> Pronounced: lah kart / luh muh-new. *La carte* = individual dishes, *le menu* = fixed price meal.

### l'entrée / le plat principal / le dessert
> *starter / main course / dessert*
> Pronounced: lahn-tray / luh plah pran-see-pal / luh deh-sehr. The three courses.

### Je vais prendre...
> *I'll have... / I'm going to have...*
> Pronounced: zhuh vay prahndr. Common way to order your food.

### l'eau / le vin / la bière
> *water / wine / beer*
> Pronounced: loh / luh van / lah byehr. Common drinks.

### C'était délicieux !
> *It was delicious!*
> Pronounced: say-tay day-lee-syuh. Compliment the food!

### L'addition, s'il vous plaît
> *The bill, please*
> Pronounced: lah-dee-syohn, seel voo pleh. When you're ready to pay.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Ordering Food

💡 At a French restaurant, meals typically have three courses:

1. **L'entrée** — starter/appetizer
2. **Le plat principal** — main course
3. **Le dessert** — dessert

To order, use:

**Je vais prendre...** or **Je voudrais...**

Examples:
- Je vais prendre une salade. = I'll have a salad.
- Je voudrais le poulet. = I would like the chicken.

🗣️ Tip: In France, service is usually included in the price. Tipping is optional but leaving small change is appreciated!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **le serveur / la serveuse** — *waiter / waitress*
- **bon appétit** — *enjoy your meal* (what French people say before eating)
- **à point / bien cuit** — *medium / well done* (for meat)
- **Je suis végétarien(ne)** — *I'm vegetarian* (masc./fem.)
- **C'est pour moi** — *It's on me / I'm paying*

quizPairs.question: Une table pour... personnes, s'il vous plaît
the menu (à la carte) / the set menu
l'entrée / le plat principal / le dessert

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 15: Review & Practice (Lessons 11-14)

Welcome to Lesson 15! Today we'll review everything from Lessons 11-14 and practice combining all your knowledge in longer, more natural conversations.

---

## 🔤 Review: Key Topics

### Places & Directions (Lesson 11)
- Où est la gare ? — Where is the station?
- à gauche / à droite / tout droit — left / right / straight
- Excusez-moi... — Excuse me...
- près de / loin de — near / far from

### Weather & Seasons (Lesson 12)
- Quel temps fait-il ? — What's the weather like?
- Il fait beau / chaud / froid — It's nice / hot / cold
- Il pleut / Il neige — It's raining / snowing
- les quatre saisons — the four seasons

### Hobbies & Free Time (Lesson 13)
- J'aime / Je n'aime pas... — I like / I don't like...
- le sport / la musique / la lecture — sports / music / reading
- jouer à / jouer de — to play (sport) / to play (instrument)
- le week-end / avec mes amis — weekend / with my friends

### At the Restaurant (Lesson 14)
- Une table pour... — A table for...
- l'entrée / le plat / le dessert — starter / main / dessert
- Je vais prendre... — I'll have...
- L'addition, s'il vous plaît — The bill, please

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Putting It All Together

💡 You can now combine topics in one conversation:

**Example:**
- *Aujourd'hui il fait beau. On va au parc?*
  Today it's nice. Shall we go to the park?
- *Bonne idée! Après, on peut aller au restaurant.*
  Good idea! After, we can go to a restaurant.

🗣️ Tip: Don't be afraid to make mistakes. Communication is more important than perfection!

---

## 🎁 Bonus: Conversation Connectors

- **Alors...** — *So... / Then...*
- **Et toi ? / Et vous ?** — *And you?*
- **Vraiment ?** — *Really?*
- **Ah bon ?** — *Oh really?*
- **Bien sûr !** — *Of course!*
- **Pas de problème !** — *No problem!*

## Lesson 16: Colors & Clothing

Welcome to Lesson 16! Today you'll learn colors and clothing vocabulary in French — perfect for shopping, describing what you wear, and talking about style.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### les couleurs de base
> *basic colors*
> Pronounced: lay koo-lur duh bahz.
> - rouge (red) / bleu (blue) / vert (green)
> - jaune (yellow) / noir (black) / blanc (white)

### un vêtement / des vêtements
> *an item of clothing / clothes*
> Pronounced: uh vet-mahn / day vet-mahn. General words for clothing.

### un pantalon / une robe / un t-shirt
> *pants / a dress / a t-shirt*
> Pronounced: uh pahn-tah-lohn / oon rob / uh tee-shurt. Essential clothing items.

### une chemise / un pull / une veste
> *a shirt / a sweater / a jacket*
> Pronounced: oon shuh-meez / uh pull / oon vest. More wardrobe basics.

### Je porte... / Il porte...
> *I wear / He wears...*
> Pronounced: zhuh port / eel port. Use to describe what someone is wearing.

### de quelle couleur ?
> *what color?*
> Pronounced: duh kel koo-lur. Example: *De quelle couleur est ta robe?*

### C'est trop grand / petit
> *It's too big / small*
> Pronounced: say troh grahn / puh-tee. Useful when trying on clothes.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Color Agreement

💡 Most French colors **agree with the noun** they describe:

| Color | Masculine | Feminine | Example |
|-------|-----------|----------|---------|
| blue | bleu | bleue | un pull bleu / une robe bleue |
| green | vert | verte | un pantalon vert / une veste verte |
| white | blanc | blanche | un t-shirt blanc / une chemise blanche |

🗣️ Exceptions: *marron* (brown) and *orange* don't change!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **essayer** — *to try on* (e.g., *Je peux l'essayer ?* = Can I try it on?)
- **la taille** — *size* (e.g., *Quelle taille faites-vous ?* = What size are you?)
- **cher / chère** — *expensive* (masc./fem.)
- **bon marché** — *inexpensive / good value*
- **J'aime bien... / Je préfère...** — *I quite like... / I prefer...*

quizPairs.question: un pantalon / une robe / un t-shirt

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 17: Body Parts & Health

Welcome to Lesson 17! Today you'll learn vocabulary for body parts and how to talk about basic health — useful for describing how you feel or visiting a doctor.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### la tête / le visage / les yeux
> *the head / the face / the eyes*
> Pronounced: lah tet / luh vee-zahj / lay zyuh. Key parts of the head.

### la bouche / le nez / les oreilles
> *the mouth / the nose / the ears*
> Pronounced: lah boosh / luh nay / lay zo-ray. More facial features.

### la main / le bras / la jambe
> *the hand / the arm / the leg*
> Pronounced: lah mahn / luh brah / lah zhahmb. Limbs vocabulary.

### J'ai mal à...
> *I have pain in... / My ... hurts*
> Pronounced: zhay mal ah. Example: *J'ai mal à la tête* = I have a headache.

### Je suis malade / en forme
> *I'm sick / I'm feeling well*
> Pronounced: zhuh swee mah-lahd / ahn form. Basic health states.

### un médecin / un hôpital
> *a doctor / a hospital*
> Pronounced: uh mayd-sahn / uh noh-pee-tal. Healthcare vocabulary.

### Ça va mieux / Ça ne va pas
> *I'm feeling better / I'm not feeling well*
> Pronounced: sah vah myuh / sah nuh vah pah. Describing your condition.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Talking About Pain

💡 To say something hurts, use:

**J'ai mal à + [body part]**

Examples:
- J'ai mal à la tête. = I have a headache.
- J'ai mal au bras. = My arm hurts. (*à + le = au*)
- J'ai mal aux jambes. = My legs hurt. (*à + les = aux*)

🗣️ Note the contractions: *à + le = au*, *à + les = aux*!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **avoir de la fièvre** — *to have a fever*
- **tousser / éternuer** — *to cough / to sneeze*
- **se reposer** — *to rest* (reflexive verb)
- **prendre un médicament** — *to take medicine*
- **Ça va passer** — *It will pass / You'll be fine*

quizPairs.question: la tête / le visage / les yeux
quizPairs.answer: the head / the face / the eyes

quizPairs.question: la bouche / le nez / les oreilles
quizPairs.answer: the mouth / the nose / the ears

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 18: Travel & Transportation

Welcome to Lesson 18! Today you'll learn essential vocabulary for traveling in French-speaking countries — from getting around town to booking tickets and asking for help.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### le train / le bus / le métro
> *the train / the bus / the subway*
> Pronounced: luh trahn / luh boos / luh may-troh. Common public transport.

### un billet / un ticket
> *a ticket (long-distance) / a ticket (local)*
> Pronounced: uh bee-yay / uh tee-kay. Use *billet* for trains, *ticket* for metro/bus.

### Je voudrais aller à...
> *I would like to go to...*
> Pronounced: zhuh voo-dreh ah-lay ah. Essential for stating your destination.

### Où va ce bus / ce train ?
> *Where does this bus/train go?*
> Pronounced: oo vah suh boos / suh trahn. Helpful for confirming your route.

### à quelle heure part... ?
> *at what time does ... leave?*
> Pronounced: ah kel ur par. Example: *À quelle heure part le train?*

### la gare / l'arrêt de bus
> *the train station / the bus stop*
> Pronounced: lah gar / lah-rah deh boos. Where you catch transport.

### Combien coûte un billet pour... ?
> *How much is a ticket to...?*
> Pronounced: kohm-byahn koot uh bee-yay poor. Essential for buying tickets.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Asking About Transport

💡 To ask about departure times:

**À quelle heure part + [transport]?**

Examples:
- À quelle heure part le train? = What time does the train leave?
- À quelle heure part le bus? = What time does the bus leave?

To ask about destinations:
**Où va ce...?**
- Où va ce bus? = Where does this bus go?

🗣️ Tip: In France, always validate your ticket (*composter*) before boarding trains!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **un aller simple / un aller-retour** — *a one-way ticket / a round-trip ticket*
- **la sortie / l'entrée** — *exit / entrance* (for stations)
- **en retard / à l'heure** — *late / on time*
- **Je suis perdu(e)** — *I'm lost* (masc./fem.)
- **Pouvez-vous m'aider ?** — *Can you help me?* (polite request)

quizPairs.question: le train / le bus / le métro
quizPairs.answer: the train / the bus / the subway

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 19: Emotions & Feelings

Welcome to Lesson 19! Today you'll learn how to express emotions and feelings in French — essential for deeper conversations and connecting with others.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Je suis heureux / heureuse
> *I'm happy* (masc. / fem.)
> Pronounced: zhuh swee zuh-ruh / uh-ruhz. Add 'e' for feminine form.

### Je suis triste / fatigué(e)
> *I'm sad / tired* (masc. / fem.)
> Pronounced: zhuh swee treest / fah-tee-gay. Basic emotional states.

### Je suis en colère / stressé(e)
> *I'm angry / stressed* (masc. / fem.)
> Pronounced: zhuh swee ahn koh-lehr / streh-say. Stronger emotions.

### Comment tu te sens ?
> *How do you feel?* (informal)
> Pronounced: koh-mahn too tuh sahn. A caring way to ask about someone.

### Je me sens...
> *I feel...*
> Pronounced: zhuh muh sahn. Example: *Je me sens bien* = I feel good.

### Ça me rend heureux / triste
> *That makes me happy / sad*
> Pronounced: sah muh rahN zuh-ruh / treest. Expressing cause of feelings.

### Ne t'inquiète pas !
> *Don't worry!* (informal)
> Pronounced: nuh tan-kyet pah. Reassuring someone.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Reflexive Verbs for Feelings

💡 To talk about how you feel, use **se sentir** (to feel):

| Pronoun | Conjugation | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| je | me sens | Je me sens bien. |
| tu | te sens | Comment te sens-tu? |
| il/elle | se sent | Il se sent triste. |
| nous | nous sentons | Nous nous sentons fatigués. |
| vous | vous sentez | Vous vous sentez mieux? |
| ils/elles | se sentent | Ils se sentent heureux. |

🗣️ Tip: The reflexive pronoun changes with the subject!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **excité(e)** — *excited* (masc./fem.)
- **nerveux / nerveuse** — *nervous* (masc./fem.)
- **fier / fière** — *proud* (masc./fem.)
- **Je suis content(e) de...** — *I'm glad to...* (e.g., *de te voir* = to see you)
- **Courage !** — *Hang in there! / You can do it!* (encouragement)

## Lesson 20: Review & Practice (Lessons 16-19)

Welcome to Lesson 20 — your second milestone! Today we'll review everything from Lessons 16-19 and practice combining your knowledge in natural, real-life conversations.

---

## 🔤 Review: Key Topics

### Colors & Clothing (Lesson 16)
- rouge / bleu / vert / noir — red / blue / green / black
- un pantalon / une robe / un t-shirt — pants / dress / t-shirt
- Je porte... — I wear / I'm wearing...
- De quelle couleur ? — What color?

### Body & Health (Lesson 17)
- la tête / la main / la jambe — head / hand / leg
- J'ai mal à... — My ... hurts / I have pain in...
- Je suis malade / en forme — I'm sick / I'm well
- un médecin — a doctor

### Travel & Transport (Lesson 18)
- le train / le bus / le métro — train / bus / subway
- un billet — a ticket
- Je voudrais aller à... — I would like to go to...
- À quelle heure part... ? — What time does ... leave?

### Emotions & Feelings (Lesson 19)
- Je suis heureux / triste — I'm happy / sad
- Comment tu te sens ? — How do you feel?
- Je me sens... — I feel...
- Ne t'inquiète pas ! — Don't worry!

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Building Real Conversations

💡 You can now mix topics naturally:

**Example 1 (Shopping + Colors):**
- *Je cherche un pantalon bleu. De quelle taille?*
  I'm looking for blue pants. What size?

**Example 2 (Health + Travel):**
- *Je suis malade. Je voudrais aller à l'hôpital.*
  I'm sick. I would like to go to the hospital.

**Example 3 (Emotions + Plans):**
- *Je suis heureux! On va au cinéma ce soir?*
  I'm happy! Shall we go to the cinema tonight?

🗣️ Tip: Keep sentences simple. Communication > perfection!

---

## 🎁 Bonus: Natural Conversation Flow

- **D'abord...** — *First...*
- **Ensuite...** — *Then / Next...*
- **Après...** — *After that...*
- **Finalement...** — *Finally...*
- **En fait...** — *Actually... / In fact...*

quizPairs.question: Je suis malade. Je voudrais aller à l'hôpital.
Je suis heureux! On va au cinéma ce soir?

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 21: Introduction to Past Tense (Passé Composé with Avoir)

Welcome to Lesson 21! Today you'll take a big step forward: learning how to talk about the past in French using the *passé composé* with *avoir*.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### J'ai mangé / parlé / travaillé
> *I ate / spoke / worked*
> Pronounced: zhay mahn-zhay / par-lay / trah-vah-yay. This is the *passé composé*: **avoir** + past participle.

### Tu as regardé / écouté / visité
> *You watched / listened / visited*
> Pronounced: too ah ruh-gar-day / ay-koo-tay / vee-zee-tay. Same pattern with *tu*.

### Il/Elle a fini / pris / fait
> *He/She finished / took / did*
> Pronounced: eel/el ah fee-nee / pree / fet. Some past participles are irregular.

### Qu'est-ce que tu as fait... ?
> *What did you do...?*
> Pronounced: kes-kuh too ah feh. Essential question for talking about the past.

### Hier / La semaine dernière
> *Yesterday / Last week*
> Pronounced: yehr / lah suh-men der-nyehr. Common time markers for past events.

### C'était bien / super / intéressant
> *It was good / great / interesting*
> Pronounced: say-teh byehn / sew-pehr / an-tay-reh-sahn. Describing past experiences.

### Je n'ai pas...
> *I didn't...*
> Pronounced: zhuh nay pah. Negation in past tense: *ne* + *ai* + *pas* + participle.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Passé Composé with Avoir

💡 To form the past tense for most verbs:

**Subject + avoir (present) + past participle**

| Verb | Past Participle | Example |
|------|----------------|---------|
| parler (to speak) | parlé | J'ai parlé. |
| manger (to eat) | mangé | J'ai mangé. |
| regarder (to watch) | regardé | J'ai regardé. |
| finir (to finish) | fini | J'ai fini. |

🗣️ Regular -er verbs: drop -er, add **-é**.
🗣️ Regular -ir verbs: drop -ir, add **-i**.

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **d'abord... puis... enfin...** — *first... then... finally...* (for sequencing past events)
- **beaucoup / un peu** — *a lot / a little* (e.g., *J'ai beaucoup travaillé*)
- **vraiment** — *really* (e.g., *C'était vraiment bien*)
- **et toi ? / et vous ?** — *and you?* (to ask about someone else's past)
- **raconter** — *to tell / to recount* (e.g., *Raconte-moi !* = Tell me!)

## Lesson 22: Past Tense Practice & Common Irregular Verbs

Welcome to Lesson 22! Today you'll practice the passé composé with more verbs, including essential irregular ones you'll use every day.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### J'ai eu / j'ai été / j'ai fait
> *I had / I was / I did or made*
> Pronounced: zhay ew / zhay ay-tay / zhay fet. Three essential irregular past forms.

### Nous avons / Vous avez / Ils ont + participle
> *We / You (pl.) / They + past action*
> Pronounced: noo zah-vohn / voo zah-vay / eel zohn. Same pattern, different subjects.

### Je suis allé(e) / venu(e)
> *I went / I came* (verbs that use *être*, not *avoir*)
> Pronounced: zhuh swee zah-lay / vuh-new. Note: these agree with gender!

### Qu'est-ce qu'il/elle a fait ?
> *What did he/she do?*
> Pronounced: kes-kuh eel/el ah feh. Asking about third person past actions.

### Il y a + time
> *...ago*
> Pronounced: eel yah. Example: *Il y a deux jours* = two days ago.

### Déjà / encore / jamais (in past)
> *already / still or again / never*
> Pronounced: day-zhah / ahn-kor / zhah-meh. Useful adverbs with past tense.

### Raconte-moi !
> *Tell me about it!*
> Pronounced: rah-kohnt mwah. Casual way to ask for a story.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Key Irregular Past Participles

💡 Memorize these common irregular past participles:

| Infinitive | Past Participle | Example |
|------------|-----------------|---------|
| avoir (to have) | eu | J'ai eu. (I had) |
| être (to be) | été | J'ai été. (I was) |
| faire (to do/make) | fait | J'ai fait. (I did) |
| prendre (to take) | pris | J'ai pris. (I took) |
| voir (to see) | vu | J'ai vu. (I saw) |
| pouvoir (to be able) | pu | J'ai pu. (I could) |

🗣️ Verbs that use *être* (like *aller*, *venir*) agree with the subject:
- Je suis allé (male) / Je suis allée (female)

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **tout de suite** — *right away* (e.g., *Je l'ai fait tout de suite*)
- **pendant** — *during / for* (e.g., *pendant une heure* = for one hour)
- **ensuite** — *then / next* (sequencing past events)
- **finalement** — *finally / in the end*
- **C'était comment ?** — *How was it?* (asking for description)

quizPairs.question: J'ai eu / j'ai été / j'ai fait
quizPairs.answer: I had / I was / I did or made

quizPairs.question: Nous avons / Vous avez / Ils ont + participle

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 23: Near Future (Futur Proche)

Welcome to Lesson 23! Today you'll learn how to talk about your immediate plans and intentions using the *futur proche* — the easiest way to express the future in French.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Je vais + [infinitive]
> *I am going to...*
> Pronounced: zhuh vay. Example: *Je vais manger* = I'm going to eat.

### Tu vas / Il va / Nous allons / Vous allez / Ils vont
> *You / He / We / You (pl.) / They are going to...*
> Pronounced: too vah / eel vah / noo zah-lohn / voo zah-lay / eel vohn. Conjugations of *aller*.

### Qu'est-ce que tu vas faire... ?
> *What are you going to do...?*
> Pronounced: kes-kuh too vah fehr. Essential question for future plans.

### Demain / Ce soir / La semaine prochaine
> *Tomorrow / Tonight / Next week*
> Pronounced: duh-mahn / suh swar / lah suh-men pro-shen. Common future time markers.

### Je ne vais pas...
> *I am not going to...*
> Pronounced: zhuh nuh vay pah. Negation with futur proche.

### On va + [infinitive]
> *We are going to... / Let's...*
> Pronounced: ohn vah. Very common in spoken French.

### C'est prévu / C'est annulé
> *It's planned / It's cancelled*
> Pronounced: say pray-vew / say ah-new-lay. Useful for confirming plans.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Futur Proche Formula

💡 To talk about the near future:

**Subject + aller (present) + infinitive verb**

| Subject | Aller | Example |
|---------|-------|---------|
| je | vais | Je vais parler. |
| tu | vas | Tu vas manger. |
| il/elle | va | Il va finir. |
| nous | allons | Nous allons visiter. |
| vous | allez | Vous allez regarder. |
| ils/elles | vont | Ils vont travailler. |

🗣️ This is exactly like English "going to" + verb!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **bientôt** — *soon* (e.g., *Je vais le faire bientôt*)
- **plus tard** — *later* (e.g., *On en parle plus tard*)
- **tout à l'heure** — *in a little while / earlier* (context-dependent)
- **peut-être** — *maybe* (e.g., *Je vais peut-être venir*)
- **J'ai hâte !** — *I can't wait!* (expressing excitement)

quizPairs.question: Tu vas / Il va / Nous allons / Vous allez / Ils vont
quizPairs.answer: You / He / We / You (pl.) / They are going to...

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 24: Negation Beyond "Ne...Pas"

Welcome to Lesson 24! Today you'll expand your ability to express negation in French with *ne...jamais*, *ne...rien*, *ne...plus*, and more.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Je ne... jamais
> *I never...*
> Pronounced: zhuh nuh zhah-meh. Example: *Je ne mange jamais de viande* = I never eat meat.

### Je ne... rien
> *I nothing / I don't... anything*
> Pronounced: zhuh nuh ryehn. Example: *Je ne vois rien* = I don't see anything.

### Je ne... plus
> *I no longer / I don't... anymore*
> Pronounced: zhuh nuh plew. Example: *Je ne fume plus* = I don't smoke anymore.

### Je ne... personne
> *I no one / I don't... anyone*
> Pronounced: zhuh nuh pehr-sonn. Example: *Je ne vois personne* = I don't see anyone.

### Ne... que
> *only*
> Pronounced: nuh kuh. Example: *Je ne mange que des légumes* = I only eat vegetables.

### Placement rule
> *ne + verb + negative word*
> In simple tenses, the negative word goes after the verb. In compound tenses (like passé composé), it goes after the auxiliary.

### Short answers
> *Moi non plus / Moi si*
> Pronounced: mwah nohn plew / mwah see. Meaning: "Me neither" / "Yes I do" (contradicting a negative).

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Negation Patterns

💡 Basic negation structure:

**ne + verb + negative word**

| Negative | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| ne...pas | not | Je ne parle pas. |
| ne...jamais | never | Je ne voyage jamais. |
| ne...rien | nothing | Je ne comprends rien. |
| ne...plus | no longer | Je ne travaille plus ici. |
| ne...personne | no one | Je ne connais personne. |
| ne...que | only | Je ne bois que de l'eau. |

🗣️ In spoken French, *ne* is often dropped: *Je sais pas* instead of *Je ne sais pas*.

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **aucun / aucune** — *no / not any* (e.g., *Je n'ai aucune idée* = I have no idea)
- **nulle part** — *nowhere* (e.g., *Je ne vais nulle part* = I'm not going anywhere)
- **pas du tout** — *not at all* (strong negation)
- **ni... ni...** — *neither... nor...* (e.g., *Je ne mange ni viande ni poisson*)
- **Quoi ? / Pardon ?** — *What? / Sorry?* (asking for repetition)

quizPairs.question: Je ne... plus
quizPairs.answer: I no longer / I don't... anymore

quizPairs.question: Je ne... personne
quizPairs.answer: I no one / I don't... anyone

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 25: Direct Object Pronouns (le, la, les)

Welcome to Lesson 25! Today you'll learn how to replace nouns with direct object pronouns — a key step toward sounding more natural and fluent in French.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### le / la / les
> *him / her / it (masc.) / her / it (fem.) / them*
> Pronounced: luh / lah / lay. These replace direct objects (things or people receiving the action).

### Je le / la / les + verb
> *I + pronoun + verb*
> Pronounced: zhuh luh / lah / lay. Example: *Je le mange* = I eat it.

### Tu le / la / les + verb
> *You + pronoun + verb*
> Pronounced: too luh / lah / lay. Example: *Tu la connais* = You know her.

### Placement rule
> *pronoun before the verb*
> In simple tenses, the pronoun goes directly before the verb. In futur proche or with two verbs, it goes before the infinitive.

### Je ne le / la / les + verb + pas
> *I don't + pronoun + verb*
> Pronounced: zhuh nuh luh / lah / lay... pah. Negation wraps around the pronoun + verb.

### L' before vowel
> *le/la become l' before a vowel*
> Example: *Je l'aime* = I love him/her/it. Pronounced: zhuh lem.

### Affirmative responses
> *Oui, je le fais / Non, je ne le fais pas*
> Pronounced: wee zhuh luh feh / nohn zhuh nuh luh feh pah. Confirming or denying with pronouns.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Direct Object Pronouns

💡 Use **le, la, les** to replace a noun that directly receives the action:

| Noun | Pronoun | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| le livre (masc.) | le | Je lis le livre → Je le lis. |
| la pomme (fem.) | la | Je mange la pomme → Je la mange. |
| les amis (plural) | les | Je vois les amis → Je les vois. |

🗣️ Placement: Always before the verb (except in affirmative commands).

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **lui / leur** — *to him / to her / to them* (indirect objects — coming soon!)
- **y** — *there* (e.g., *J'y vais* = I'm going there)
- **en** — *some / any of it* (e.g., *J'en veux* = I want some)
- **C'est ça !** — *That's it! / Exactly!* (confirmation)
- **Je m'en souviens** — *I remember it* (with pronoun *en*)

quizPairs.question: le / la / les
quizPairs.answer: him / her / it (masc.) / her / it (fem.) / them

quizPairs.question: L' before vowel
quizPairs.answer: le/la become l' before a vowel

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 26: Home & Furniture

Welcome to Lesson 26! Today you'll learn vocabulary for talking about your home, rooms, and furniture — essential for describing where you live and what's inside.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### la maison / l'appartement
> *the house / the apartment*
> Pronounced: lah meh-zohn / lah-par-tuh-mahn. Two main types of homes.

### la pièce / la chambre / le salon
> *the room / the bedroom / the living room*
> Pronounced: lah pyehs / lah shahmbr / luh sah-lohn. Common rooms in a home.

### la cuisine / la salle de bain
> *the kitchen / the bathroom*
> Pronounced: lah kwee-zeen / lah sahl duh bahN. Essential rooms.

### un lit / une table / une chaise
> *a bed / a table / a chair*
> Pronounced: uh lee / oon tahbl / oon shehz. Basic furniture items.

### un canapé / une armoire / une étagère
> *a sofa / a wardrobe / a shelf*
> Pronounced: uh kah-nah-pay / oon ar-mwar / oon ay-tah-zhehr. More home vocabulary.

### Il y a... / Il n'y a pas de...
> *There is/are... / There isn't/aren't any...*
> Pronounced: eel yah / eel nee yah pah duh. Describing what's in a room.

### Où est... ? / Où sont... ?
> *Where is...? / Where are...?*
> Pronounced: oo ay / oo sohn. Asking about location of items.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Describing Your Home

💡 Use **Il y a** to say what exists in a place:

| French | English | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Il y a un lit | There is a bed | Dans ma chambre, il y a un lit. |
| Il y a des chaises | There are chairs | Dans la cuisine, il y a des chaises. |
| Il n'y a pas de... | There isn't/aren't any... | Il n'y a pas de télévision. |

🗣️ Note: After *pas de*, the article disappears: *pas de télévision* (not *pas une télévision*).

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **le balcon / le jardin / la terrasse** — *balcony / garden / terrace*
- **confortable / spacieux / petit(e)** — *comfortable / spacious / small*
- **au premier étage / au rez-de-chaussée** — *on the first floor / on the ground floor*
- **Je loue / J'achète** — *I rent / I buy* (talking about housing)
- **C'est chez moi** — *This is my place / It's at my home*

quizPairs.question: la pièce / la chambre / le salon
quizPairs.answer: the room / the bedroom / the living room

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 27: Professions & Work

Welcome to Lesson 27! Today you'll learn how to talk about jobs, professions, and daily work life in French — useful for introductions and professional conversations.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Je suis + [profession]
> *I am a...*
> Pronounced: zhuh swee. Example: *Je suis médecin* = I am a doctor. Note: no article before profession!

### un médecin / un professeur / un ingénieur
> *a doctor / a teacher / an engineer*
> Pronounced: uh mayd-sahn / uh pro-feh-sur / uh ahn-zhay-nyur. Common professions.

### une infirmière / une avocate / une journaliste
> *a nurse / a lawyer / a journalist* (feminine forms)
> Pronounced: oon an-feer-myehr / oon ah-voh-kah / oon zhur-nah-leest. Add -e for feminine.

### Je travaille comme... / dans...
> *I work as... / in...*
> Pronounced: zhuh trah-vahy kohm / dahN. Example: *Je travaille comme professeur*.

### un bureau / une entreprise / un hôpital
> *an office / a company / a hospital*
> Pronounced: uh bew-roh / oon ahn-truh-preez / uh noh-pee-tal. Workplaces.

### À temps plein / À temps partiel
> *Full-time / Part-time*
> Pronounced: ah tahN plahN / ah tahN par-syehl. Work schedule terms.

### Je cherche un travail / un emploi
> *I'm looking for a job*
> Pronounced: zhuh shersh uhN trah-vahy / uhN ahN-plwah. Useful for job hunting.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Professions & Gender

💡 In French, many professions change form for gender:

| Masculine | Feminine | English |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| un professeur | une professeure | a teacher |
| un étudiant | une étudiante | a student |
| un avocat | une avocate | a lawyer |
| un infirmier | une infirmière | a nurse |

🗣️ But some stay the same: *un médecin / une médecin*, *un journaliste / une journaliste*.

💡 Important: After *Je suis*, we usually omit the article:
- ✅ Je suis médecin. (NOT: Je suis un médecin — unless emphasizing)

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **le chômage / le salaire / les collègues** — *unemployment / salary / colleagues*
- **Je suis à la retraite** — *I'm retired*
- **Je fais des études** — *I'm studying*
- **Qu'est-ce que tu fais dans la vie ?** — *What do you do for a living?*
- **C'est passionnant / difficile / bien payé** — *It's exciting / difficult / well-paid*

quizPairs.question: un médecin / un professeur / un ingénieur
quizPairs.answer: a doctor / a teacher / an engineer

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 28: Meals & Eating Habits

Welcome to Lesson 28! Today you'll learn vocabulary for meals, food habits, and talking about what and when you eat — perfect for daily conversations.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### le petit-déjeuner / le déjeuner / le dîner
> *breakfast / lunch / dinner*
> Pronounced: luh puh-tee day-zhuh-nay / luh day-zhuh-nay / luh dee-nay. The three main meals.

### Je prends mon petit-déjeuner à...
> *I have my breakfast at...*
> Pronounced: zhuh prahn mohn puh-tee day-zhuh-nay ah. Use *prendre* for meals.

### le matin / à midi / le soir
> *in the morning / at noon / in the evening*
> Pronounced: luh mah-tahN / ah mee-dee / luh swahr. Meal times.

### J'aime / Je préfère / Je déteste
> *I like / I prefer / I hate*
> Pronounced: zhem / zhuh pray-fehr / zhuh day-test. Expressing food preferences.

### Je suis végétarien(ne) / vegan
> *I'm vegetarian / vegan*
> Pronounced: zhuh swee vay-zhay-tah-ryehn / vee-gahn. Dietary preferences.

### Qu'est-ce qu'il y a au menu ?
> *What's on the menu?*
> Pronounced: kes-keel yah oh muh-new. Asking about meal options.

### J'ai faim / J'ai soif
> *I'm hungry / I'm thirsty*
> Pronounced: zhay fahN / zhay swaf. Basic needs before eating.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Talking About Meals

💡 Use **prendre** (to take) for meals, not *manger*:

| French | English | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Je prends mon petit-déjeuner | I have breakfast | Je le prends à 8 heures. |
| On prend le déjeuner ensemble | We have lunch together | On le prend à midi. |
| Ils prennent le dîner tard | They have dinner late | Ils le prennent à 21h. |

🗣️ *Manger* = to eat food. *Prendre* = to have a meal.

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **le goûter** — *afternoon snack* (especially for kids)
- **sain / équilibré / rapide** — *healthy / balanced / quick* (describing meals)
- **Je cuisine / Je commande** — *I cook / I order* (takeout)
- **à la maison / au restaurant** — *at home / at a restaurant*
- **Bon appétit !** — *Enjoy your meal!* (said before eating)

quizPairs.question: le petit-déjeuner / le déjeuner / le dîner

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 29: Shopping & Sizes

Welcome to Lesson 29! Today you'll learn essential vocabulary for shopping for clothes, asking about sizes, and making purchases — practical for travel and daily life.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Quelle taille faites-vous ? / Je fais du...
> *What size are you? / I wear size...*
> Pronounced: kel tahy fet-voo / zhuh feh dew. Example: *Je fais du 40* = I wear size 40.

### S / M / L / XL
> *Small / Medium / Large / Extra Large*
> Pronounced: ess / em / el / ex-el. International sizes used in French too.

### Je peux l'essayer ? / Où sont les cabines ?
> *Can I try it on? / Where are the fitting rooms?*
> Pronounced: zhuh puh lay-seh-yay / oo sohn lay kah-been. Essential shopping questions.

### Il me va bien / Il est trop grand
> *It fits me well / It's too big*
> Pronounced: eel muh vah byehn / eel ay troh grahn. Describing fit.

### Je le/la prends / Je ne le prends pas
> *I'll take it / I won't take it*
> Pronounced: zhuh luh/lah prahn / zhuh nuh luh/lah prahn pah. Making a decision.

### C'est combien ? / Ça fait...
> *How much is it? / That comes to...*
> Pronounced: say kohm-byahn / sah feh. Talking about price.

### Je paie en espèces / par carte
> *I pay in cash / by card*
> Pronounced: zhuh peh ahN es-pehs / par kart. Payment methods.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Sizes & Fit

💡 French clothing sizes differ from US/UK:

| French | US Women | US Men |
|--------|----------|--------|
| 36 | 4 | — |
| 38 | 6 | — |
| 40 | 8 | 40 |
| 42 | 10 | 42 |

🗣️ To ask your size: **Quelle taille faites-vous?** (formal) or **Tu fais quelle taille?** (informal)
🗣️ To answer: **Je fais du 40** (clothes) or **Je chausse du 42** (shoes)

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **la caisse / le vendeur / la vendeuse** — *checkout / salesman / saleswoman*
- **une réduction / les soldes** — *a discount / the sales*
- **C'est trop cher / C'est bon marché** — *It's too expensive / It's a good deal*
- **Je cherche... / Je regarde juste** — *I'm looking for... / I'm just browsing*
- **Merci, c'est tout** — *Thanks, that's all* (when done shopping)

quizPairs.question: Quelle taille faites-vous ? / Je fais du...
quizPairs.answer: What size are you? / I wear size...

quizPairs.question: Je peux l'essayer ? / Où sont les cabines ?
quizPairs.answer: Can I try it on? / Where are the fitting rooms?

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 30: Review & Practice (Lessons 21-29)

Welcome to Lesson 30 — your third milestone! Today we'll review everything from Lessons 21-29 and practice combining your knowledge in natural, multi-topic conversations.

---

## 🔤 Review: Key Topics

### Past Tense (Lessons 21-22)
- J'ai mangé / parlé / travaillé — I ate / spoke / worked
- Qu'est-ce que tu as fait hier? — What did you do yesterday?
- Je suis allé(e) — I went (with être)
- C'était bien / super — It was good / great

### Future & Negation (Lessons 23-24)
- Je vais + infinitive — I am going to...
- Demain / la semaine prochaine — Tomorrow / next week
- Je ne... jamais / plus / rien — I never / no longer / nothing

### Pronouns & Home (Lessons 25-26)
- le / la / les — him / her / it / them (direct objects)
- Il y a... / Il n'y a pas de... — There is / There isn't
- la maison / la cuisine / le salon — house / kitchen / living room

### Work, Food & Shopping (Lessons 27-29)
- Je suis médecin / Je travaille comme... — I am a doctor / I work as...
- Je prends mon petit-déjeuner — I have breakfast
- Quelle taille faites-vous? / Je le prends — What size? / I'll take it

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Mixing Tenses Naturally

💡 You can now combine past, present, and future in one conversation:

**Example:**
- *Hier, j'ai travaillé. Demain, je vais me reposer.*
  Yesterday, I worked. Tomorrow, I'm going to rest.

**Example:**
- *Je suis professeur. J'aime mon travail. La semaine prochaine, je vais voyager.*
  I'm a teacher. I like my job. Next week, I'm going to travel.

🗣️ Tip: Keep sentences short. One idea per sentence = clearer French!

---

## 🎁 Bonus: Conversation Connectors

- **En fait...** — *Actually... / In fact...*
- **D'ailleurs...** — *Besides... / By the way...*
- **Par contre...** — *However... / On the other hand...*
- **Du coup...** — *So... / As a result...* (very common in spoken French)
- **En bref...** — *In short... / To sum up...*

## Lesson 31: Travel & Hotels

Welcome to Lesson 31! Today you'll learn essential vocabulary for booking hotels, checking in, and handling common travel situations in French-speaking countries.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Je voudrais réserver une chambre
> *I would like to book a room*
> Pronounced: zhuh voo-dreh ray-zehr-ver oon shahmbr. Essential for hotel bookings.

### une chambre simple / double / avec petit-déjeuner
> *a single / double / room with breakfast*
> Pronounced: oon shahmbr sam-pluh / doobl / ah-vek puh-tee day-zhuh-nay. Room types.

### Pour combien de nuits ? / Pour quelle date ?
> *For how many nights? / For what date?*
> Pronounced: poor kohm-byahn duh nwee / poor kel dat. Common booking questions.

### L'arrivée / Le départ
> *Check-in / Check-out*
> Pronounced: lah-ree-vay / luh day-par. Hotel timing terms.

### La clé / La carte d'accès
> *The key / The key card*
> Pronounced: lah klay / lah kart dahk-seh. Getting into your room.

### Où est le petit-déjeuner servi ?
> *Where is breakfast served?*
> Pronounced: oo ay luh puh-tee day-zhuh-nay sehr-vee. Useful morning question.

### Y a-t-il... ? / Est-ce qu'il y a... ?
> *Is there...? / Are there...?*
> Pronounced: yah-teel / ess keel yah. Asking about amenities: *Y a-t-il un ascenseur?* (Is there an elevator?)

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Hotel Questions

💡 To ask if something exists at a hotel:

**Y a-t-il + [noun] ?** (formal) or **Est-ce qu'il y a + [noun] ?** (neutral)

| French | English | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Y a-t-il un ascenseur ? | Is there an elevator? | Pour accéder au 3ème étage. |
| Est-ce qu'il y a le Wi-Fi ? | Is there Wi-Fi? | Gratuit ou payant? |
| Y a-t-il un parking ? | Is there parking? | Pour ma voiture. |

🗣️ Tip: Add *s'il vous plaît* to be polite: *Y a-t-il un restaurant, s'il vous plaît?*

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **la réception / le concierge** — *the front desk / the concierge*
- **gratuit / payant** — *free / paid* (e.g., *Le Wi-Fi est gratuit*)
- **le coffre-fort / la climatisation** — *safe / air conditioning*
- **Je voudrais une chambre calme** — *I would like a quiet room*
- **C'est pour une personne / deux personnes** — *It's for one person / two people*

quizPairs.question: Je voudrais réserver une chambre
quizPairs.answer: I would like to book a room

quizPairs.question: une chambre simple / double / avec petit-déjeuner
quizPairs.answer: a single / double / room with breakfast

quizPairs.question: Pour combien de nuits ? / Pour quelle date ?
quizPairs.answer: For how many nights? / For what date?

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 30: Review & Practice (Lessons 21-29)

Welcome to Lesson 30 — your third milestone! Today we'll review everything from Lessons 21-29 and practice combining your knowledge in natural, multi-topic conversations.

---

## 🔤 Review: Key Topics

### Past Tense (Lessons 21-22)
- J'ai mangé / parlé / travaillé — I ate / spoke / worked
- Qu'est-ce que tu as fait hier? — What did you do yesterday?
- Je suis allé(e) — I went (with être)
- C'était bien / super — It was good / great

### Future & Negation (Lessons 23-24)
- Je vais + infinitive — I am going to...
- Demain / la semaine prochaine — Tomorrow / next week
- Je ne... jamais / plus / rien — I never / no longer / nothing

### Pronouns & Home (Lessons 25-26)
- le / la / les — him / her / it / them (direct objects)
- Il y a... / Il n'y a pas de... — There is / There isn't
- la maison / la cuisine / le salon — house / kitchen / living room

### Work, Food & Shopping (Lessons 27-29)
- Je suis médecin / Je travaille comme... — I am a doctor / I work as...
- Je prends mon petit-déjeuner — I have breakfast
- Quelle taille faites-vous? / Je le prends — What size? / I'll take it

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Mixing Tenses Naturally

💡 You can now combine past, present, and future in one conversation:

**Example:**
- *Hier, j'ai travaillé. Demain, je vais me reposer.*
  Yesterday, I worked. Tomorrow, I'm going to rest.

**Example:**
- *Je suis professeur. J'aime mon travail. La semaine prochaine, je vais voyager.*
  I'm a teacher. I like my job. Next week, I'm going to travel.

🗣️ Tip: Keep sentences short. One idea per sentence = clearer French!

---

## 🎁 Bonus: Conversation Connectors

- **En fait...** — *Actually... / In fact...*
- **D'ailleurs...** — *Besides... / By the way...*
- **Par contre...** — *However... / On the other hand...*
- **Du coup...** — *So... / As a result...* (very common in spoken French)
- **En bref...** — *In short... / To sum up...*

## Lesson 33: Emotions & Relationships

Welcome to Lesson 33! Today you'll learn vocabulary for expressing deeper emotions, talking about relationships, and connecting with others in French.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Je suis amoureux / amoureuse de...
> *I'm in love with...* (masc. / fem.)
> Pronounced: zhuh swee zah-moo-roo / zah-moo-ruhz duh. Romantic feelings.

### un ami / une amie / un copain / une copine
> *a friend* (masc. / fem.) / *a buddy* (informal)
> Pronounced: uh nah-mee / oon nah-mee / uh koh-pahN / oon koh-peen. Types of friends.

### Je m'entends bien avec...
> *I get along well with...*
> Pronounced: zhuh mahN-tahn byehn ah-vek. Describing relationships.

### Je me sens seul(e) / heureux / triste
> *I feel lonely / happy / sad*
> Pronounced: zhuh muh sahn suhl / uh-ruh / treest. Emotional states.

### C'est important pour moi
> *It's important to me*
> Pronounced: say tahN-por-tahn poor mwah. Expressing values.

### Je tiens à toi / à vous
> *I care about you* (informal / formal)
> Pronounced: zhuh tyahn ah twah / ah voo. Showing affection.

### On se dispute / On se réconcilie
> *We argue / We make up*
> Pronounced: ohN suh dee-spyoot / ohN suh ray-kohn-see-lee. Relationship dynamics.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Expressing Affection

💡 French has nuanced ways to show care:

| Phrase | Meaning | Context |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Je t'aime | I love you | Romantic / very close family |
| Je tiens à toi | I care about you | Friends / developing relationships |
| Tu me manques | I miss you | Literal: "You are missing to me" |
| Je suis fier/fière de toi | I'm proud of you | Encouragement / support |

🗣️ Note: *Tu me manques* is backwards from English — literally "You are missing to me".

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **la confiance / le respect / la patience** — *trust / respect / patience*
- **Je suis jaloux / jalouse** — *I'm jealous* (masc./fem.)
- **On sort ensemble** — *We're dating* (literally: "We go out together")
- **C'est compliqué** — *It's complicated* (relationships)
- **Parlons-en !** — *Let's talk about it!* (opening communication)

quizPairs.question: un ami / une amie / un copain / une copine
Je tiens à toi / à vous

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 34: French Culture & Traditions

Welcome to Lesson 34! Today you'll learn about French culture, holidays, and social customs — essential for connecting with French speakers and understanding context.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### Joyeux Noël / Bonne année / Joyeux anniversaire
> *Merry Christmas / Happy New Year / Happy Birthday*
> Pronounced: zhwa-yuh noh-el / bun ah-nay / zhwa-yuh ah-nee-vehr-sehr. Key celebrations.

### La fête nationale / Le 14 juillet
> *National holiday / Bastille Day (July 14)*
> Pronounced: lah fet nah-syo-nal / luh kah-torz zhwee-yeh. France's main holiday.

### On fait la bise / On se serre la main
> *We kiss on cheeks / We shake hands*
> Pronounced: ohN feh lah beez / ohN suh sehr lah mahN. French greetings.

### C'est la tradition / C'est typique
> *It's tradition / It's typical*
> Pronounced: say lah trah-dee-syohn / say tee-peek. Describing customs.

### En France, on... / Chez nous, on...
> *In France, people... / In our culture, people...*
> Pronounced: ahN frahns ohN / shay noo ohN. Talking about cultural habits.

### Apporter un cadeau / Offrir des fleurs
> *Bring a gift / Offer flowers*
> Pronounced: ah-por-tay uhN kah-doh / oh-freer day flur. Social etiquette.

### À table ! / Bon appétit !
> *Dinner's ready! / Enjoy your meal!*
> Pronounced: ah tahbl / bohn ah-pay-tee. Mealtime phrases.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: French Social Customs

💡 Key cultural norms to know:

| Custom | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| La bise | Cheek kisses for greeting (2-4 depending on region) |
| Punctuality | Arrive 10-15 min late for social events ("le quart d'heure de politesse") |
| Dining | Keep hands on table (not lap), say "Bon appétit" before eating |
| Shopping | Always greet staff with "Bonjour" when entering a store |

🗣️ Tip: Using these customs shows respect and helps you connect!

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **la gastronomie / le vin / le fromage** — *French cuisine / wine / cheese*
- **les vacances d'été / les fêtes de fin d'année** — *summer holidays / year-end holidays*
- **C'est chic / C'est sympa / C'est formel** — *It's elegant / nice / formal*
- **Je découvre la culture française** — *I'm discovering French culture*
- **Quelles sont vos traditions ?** — *What are your traditions?* (asking others)

quizPairs.question: Joyeux Noël / Bonne année / Joyeux anniversaire
quizPairs.answer: Merry Christmas / Happy New Year / Happy Birthday

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 35: Pre-Final Review & Conversation Practice

Welcome to Lesson 35 — your pre-final milestone! Today we'll review key grammar and vocabulary from Lessons 31-34 and practice longer, natural conversations to prepare for the end of Beginner.

---

## 🔤 Review: Key Topics

### Travel & Hotels (Lesson 31)
- Je voudrais réserver une chambre — I'd like to book a room
- Y a-t-il un ascenseur ? — Is there an elevator?
- La clé / L'arrivée / Le départ — Key / Check-in / Check-out

### Health & Doctor (Lesson 32)
- Je ne me sens pas bien — I don't feel well
- J'ai mal à la tête / au ventre — I have a headache / stomachache
- Je voudrais voir un médecin — I'd like to see a doctor

### Emotions & Relationships (Lesson 33)
- Je tiens à toi — I care about you
- Tu me manques — I miss you
- Je me sens heureux / triste — I feel happy / sad

### Culture & Traditions (Lesson 34)
- Joyeux anniversaire — Happy Birthday
- On fait la bise — We kiss on cheeks (greeting)
- Bon appétit ! — Enjoy your meal!

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Putting It All Together

💡 You can now handle complex real-life scenarios:

**Scenario 1: Travel + Health**
- *Je suis à l'hôtel et je ne me sens pas bien. Y a-t-il un médecin près d'ici?*
  I'm at the hotel and I don't feel well. Is there a doctor nearby?

**Scenario 2: Culture + Relationships**
- *C'est mon anniversaire! On fait la bise et on dit "Bon appétit!"*
  It's my birthday! We kiss on cheeks and say "Enjoy your meal!"

**Scenario 3: Emotions + Future Plans**
- *Je suis heureux. Demain, je vais visiter mes amis. Ils me manquent!*
  I'm happy. Tomorrow, I'm going to visit my friends. I miss them!

🗣️ Tip: Don't aim for perfection. Clear communication > perfect grammar!

---

## 🎁 Bonus: Final Conversation Boosters

- **En résumé...** — *To sum up...*
- **Ce que je veux dire, c'est...** — *What I mean is...*
- **Si je comprends bien...** — *If I understand correctly...*
- **C'est exactement ça !** — *That's exactly it!*
- **Je progresse en français !** — *I'm making progress in French!*

quizPairs.question: Je suis à l'hôtel et je ne me sens pas bien. Y a-t-il un médecin près d'ici?
C'est mon anniversaire! On fait la bise et on dit "Bon appétit!"
Ce que je veux dire, c'est...

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 36: Indirect Object Pronouns (lui, leur)

Welcome to Lesson 36! Today you'll learn how to use indirect object pronouns — a key step toward more natural, fluent French conversations.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### lui / leur
> *to him / to her / to them*
> Pronounced: lwee / lur. These replace "à + person" after certain verbs.

### Je lui parle / Je leur téléphone
> *I speak to him/her / I call them*
> Pronounced: zhuh lwee parl / zhuh lur tay-lay-fon. Using indirect pronouns with verbs.

### Verbs that take "à": parler à, téléphoner à, répondre à, donner à
> *Common verbs requiring indirect objects*
> These verbs are followed by *à + person*, which can be replaced by *lui/leur*.

### Je ne lui dis pas / Je ne leur écris pas
> *I don't tell him/her / I don't write to them*
> Pronounced: zhuh nuh lwee dee pah / zhuh nuh lur ay-kree pah. Negation with indirect pronouns.

### Placement rule
> *pronoun before the verb*
> In simple tenses, the pronoun goes directly before the verb. In futur proche or with two verbs, it goes before the infinitive.

### C'est à lui / C'est à eux
> *It's his / It's theirs*
> Pronounced: say ah lwee / say ah uh. Expressing possession with indirect objects.

### Poser une question à quelqu'un
> *To ask someone a question*
> Example: *Je lui pose une question* = I ask him/her a question.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Indirect Object Pronouns

💡 Use **lui / leur** to replace *à + person*:

| Noun phrase | Pronoun | Example |
|-------------|---------|---------|
| à Marie | lui | Je parle à Marie → Je lui parle. |
| à Paul | lui | Je téléphone à Paul → Je lui téléphone. |
| à mes amis | leur | Je réponds à mes amis → Je leur réponds. |

🗣️ Important: *lui* works for both masculine AND feminine singular!
🗣️ Placement: Always before the verb (except in affirmative commands).

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary

- **offrir quelque chose à quelqu'un** — *to give something to someone*
- **demander quelque chose à quelqu'un** — *to ask something of someone*
- **expliquer quelque chose à quelqu'un** — *to explain something to someone*
- **Je vais lui dire / Je vais leur parler** — *I'm going to tell him/her / talk to them*
- **C'est pour toi / pour vous** — *It's for you* (informal / formal)

quizPairs.question: lui / leur
quizPairs.answer: to him / to her / to them

quizPairs.question: Je lui parle / Je leur téléphone
quizPairs.answer: I speak to him/her / I call them

lessons.markdown:
## Lesson 37: Simple Relative Pronouns (qui, que, où)

Welcome to Lesson 37! Today you'll learn how to connect ideas using simple relative pronouns — a powerful tool for building more complex, natural sentences.

---

## 🔤 Key Phrases

### qui = who / that (subject)
> *Used when the relative pronoun is the subject of the clause*
> Pronounced: kee. Example: *La femme qui parle* = The woman who is speaking.

### que = whom / that (object)
> *Used when the relative pronoun is the direct object*
> Pronounced: kuh. Example: *Le livre que je lis* = The book that I'm reading.

### où = where / when
> *Used for places or times*
> Pronounced: oo. Example: *La ville où j'habite* = The city where I live.

### C'est... qui / C'est... que
> *It is... who / It is... that (for emphasis)*
> Pronounced: say... kee / say... kuh. Example: *C'est moi qui ai fait ça* = It's me who did that.

### Placement & agreement
> *que becomes qu' before a vowel*
> Example: *L'homme qu'elle aime* = The man she loves.

### Common patterns
> *Voici / Voilà + noun + qui/que/où*
> Example: *Voilà le restaurant où nous avons dîné* = There's the restaurant where we dined.

---

## 🔦 Spotlight: Qui vs. Que vs. Où

💡 Quick guide to choosing the right pronoun:

| Pronoun | Function | Example | Translation |
|---------|----------|---------|-------------|
| qui | subject | La personne qui parle | The person who is speaking |
| que | direct object | La personne que je vois | The person whom I see |
| où | place/time | La ville où je vais | The city where I'm going |

🗣️ Tip: If you can replace with "he/she/they" + verb → use **qui**.
🗣️ If you can replace with "him/her/them" → use **que**.

---

## 🎁 Bonus Vocabulary
